postCopy.instagram: A body in the Winter Palace. A killer wearing someone else's name. 🕯️🔍 Case #042 — The Romanov Mask. Play free at thedailywhodunit.com
postCopy.tiktok: Free daily murder mystery game: someone inside the Romanov palace is not who they claim to be — and someone is dead because of it. Daily deduction puzzle set in 1916 imperial Russia. Solve it at thedailywhodunit.com

marketing.imagePrompt: Interior of the Romanov Winter Palace in 1916, vast gilded corridor lit by dying candlelight. A shattered crystal glass lies on a marble floor beside a fallen velvet curtain. Snow presses against frost-edged windows. Deep shadows pool between gilded columns. Color palette: ice blue, tarnished gold, blood red, near-black. Cinematic and painterly, style of Valentin Serov meets John Singer Sargent.
marketing.animationPrompt: Slow glide through a candlelit imperial corridor in the 1916 Winter Palace. Candle flames flicker in a sourceless draft. A velvet curtain shifts — no one is behind it. Hold on a shattered crystal glass on the marble floor, a dark liquid spreading slowly across the stone. A door at the far end closes without a sound. Fade to black.

rookie.title: The Romanov Mask
rookie.flavor: Count Nikolai Verin was found dead in the East Gallery of the Winter Palace hours before a crucial audience with the Tsar. He had been the only man who knew the true identity of a new court advisor — a man calling himself Baron Alexei Sorin. Now Verin is dead and Sorin is nowhere to be found.
rookie.emptyState: The man who knew the secret is dead. Find who needed him silent.

items.name: Baron Alexei Sorin
items.detail: The new court advisor whose true identity only Verin knew.

items.name: Countess Irina Volkov
items.detail: A senior lady of the court with ambitions for her family's restoration.

items.name: Pavel Drusin
items.detail: A palace guard captain recently transferred from the front lines.

items.name: The East Gallery
items.detail: Where Verin's body was found beneath a portrait of Alexander II.

items.name: The Amber Antechamber
items.detail: A private waiting room adjacent to the Tsar's audience hall.

items.name: The Palace Crypt
items.detail: A rarely visited lower level used for storage of state documents.

items.name: Poisoned Crystal Glass
items.detail: Sherry served in a monogrammed glass found shattered near the body.

items.name: Letter Opener
items.detail: An ornate blade from the gallery writing desk, wiped but not cleaned.

items.name: Silk Cravat
items.detail: A gentleman's neckcloth of sufficient length to strangle.

clues.heading: The False Title
clues.text: A search of palace records reveals no Baron Alexei Sorin registered in any imperial genealogical registry. The title was invented. Count Verin had filed a private notation three weeks prior flagging Sorin's credentials as fraudulent and requesting a personal audience with the Tsar's chamberlain.
clues.alone: Baron Sorin's identity is entirely fabricated — and Verin had formally flagged this before his death.

links.insight: Sorin's fabricated title confirmed in palace records and Verin's formal challenge filed three weeks prior together establish that Sorin had both the knowledge of exposure and sufficient time to plan a murder before the chamberlain acted.

clues.heading: The Sherry and the Residue
clues.text: The shattered crystal glass near Verin's body tested positive for arsenic trioxide dissolved in sherry. The decanter on the gallery's writing desk was clean. The poison was introduced into a single glass — meaning it was prepared specifically for Verin before he arrived.
clues.alone: The poison was placed in Verin's glass before he entered the gallery — this was a targeted, premeditated killing.

links.insight: The poisoned glass prepared specifically for Verin and Sorin's presence in the East Gallery that evening confirmed by the footman together place Sorin at the scene with the means and preparation to commit the killing.

clues.heading: Countess Volkov's Alibi
clues.text: Countess Irina Volkov was confirmed in the Amber Antechamber from the eighth hour of evening until well past midnight, attending a private musical recital witnessed by eleven members of the court. She did not leave the room once, confirmed by both a lady-in-waiting and the palace majordomo.
clues.alone: Countess Volkov has an unbreakable alibi — eleven witnesses place her in the Antechamber all evening.

links.insight: Countess Volkov eliminated by her eleven-witness alibi and Drusin confirmed at his post on the west wing together leave Sorin as the only suspect unaccounted for in the East Gallery during the window of death.

clues.heading: The Ambitious Letter
clues.text: A letter found in Verin's coat, unsealed, was addressed to the Tsar's chamberlain and outlined in precise detail the true identity of Baron Sorin — a Bolshevik-affiliated agitator named Dmitri Kashin, wanted by the Okhrana for seditious activity in Moscow in 1914. Verin intended to deliver it the following morning.
clues.alone: Verin was about to expose Sorin as a wanted political agitator — the letter gives Sorin an absolute motive for murder.

links.insight: The letter naming Sorin as wanted agitator Dmitri Kashin and the ink matching Sorin's writing kit found in the gallery writing desk confirm he read the letter before Verin arrived and understood what exposure would mean for his mission.

clues.heading: The Registry Forgery
clues.text: The palace genealogical registry entry for Baron Sorin was added six months ago in a different ink from surrounding entries and in a hand that does not match the registry clerk's. A cryptographer confirms the entry was interpolated — inserted into the bound volume after the fact using a forged binding repair.
clues.alone: Sorin's registry entry was physically forged and inserted — someone with access to the palace archives constructed his false identity deliberately.

links.insight: Sorin's fabricated title confirmed in palace records and Verin's formal challenge filed three weeks prior together establish that Sorin had both the knowledge of exposure and sufficient time to plan a murder before the chamberlain acted.

clues.heading: The Footman's Sighting
clues.text: A palace footman confirms he saw a man matching Baron Sorin's description entering the East Gallery at the ninth hour of evening — approximately thirty minutes before Verin's body was discovered. The footman did not see Sorin leave. No other visitors to the gallery are recorded that evening.
clues.alone: Sorin was seen entering the East Gallery before the murder and was never seen leaving — he was alone in the room with the victim.

links.insight: The poisoned glass prepared specifically for Verin and Sorin's presence in the East Gallery that evening confirmed by the footman together place Sorin at the scene with the means and preparation to commit the killing.

clues.heading: Drusin's Watch Log
clues.text: Captain Pavel Drusin's watch log for the evening of the murder shows him stationed at the west wing gate from the seventh hour through midnight without relief. Three subordinate guards countersign the log. His post is physically incompatible with access to the East Gallery during the window of death.
clues.alone: Drusin's watch log and three countersignatures confirm he was posted at the west wing throughout the evening — he could not have reached the East Gallery.

links.insight: Countess Volkov eliminated by her eleven-witness alibi and Drusin confirmed at his post on the west wing together leave Sorin as the only suspect unaccounted for in the East Gallery during the window of death.

clues.heading: The Ink Match
clues.text: The letter found in Verin's coat had been opened and resealed with a different wax — the original seal was broken. The ink used in the letter's marginalia — small notations made by someone who read it — matches the travelling ink kit found in the East Gallery writing desk, registered to Baron Sorin.
clues.alone: Someone opened Verin's letter, read it, made notes in the margins, and resealed it — using ink from Sorin's own kit.

links.insight: The letter naming Sorin as wanted agitator Dmitri Kashin and the ink matching Sorin's writing kit confirm he read the letter before Verin arrived and understood what exposure would mean for his mission.

rookie.epilogue: Dmitri Kashin — operating as Baron Alexei Sorin — had spent six months infiltrating the Romanov court with the goal of influencing imperial policy from within. When Verin's challenge threatened to expose him hours before his key audience, he intercepted the letter, prepared a poisoned glass of sherry, and met Verin in the gallery under the pretense of a private conversation. He fled through the palace's service passages before the body was found. Okhrana agents arrested him in Helsinki three weeks later.

detective.title: The Romanov Mask
detective.flavor: Verin is dead, Sorin has vanished, and the palace is locked down by imperial order. Someone inside these walls helped Sorin build his false identity — and that same person may have helped him escape. The mask goes deeper than one man.
detective.emptyState: The false baron is gone. Find what drove him — and who helped build the lie.

items.name: Baron Alexei Sorin
items.detail: The false baron — real name Dmitri Kashin, Bolshevik agitator.

items.name: Countess Irina Volkov
items.detail: Senior lady of the court, eleven-witness alibi for the evening.

items.name: Pavel Drusin
items.detail: Guard captain, west wing post confirmed all evening.

items.name: Arkady Lenz
items.detail: Palace archivist with access to the genealogical registry.

items.name: The East Gallery
items.detail: Crime scene — Verin found beneath the portrait of Alexander II.

items.name: The Amber Antechamber
items.detail: Where Countess Volkov's alibi was established all evening.

items.name: The Palace Crypt
items.detail: Lower level storage — arsenic trioxide sourced from here.

items.name: The Registry Archive
items.detail: Where Sorin's forged genealogical entry was inserted.

items.name: Poisoned Crystal Glass
items.detail: Arsenic trioxide in sherry — prepared for Verin specifically.

items.name: Letter Opener
items.detail: Ornate blade from the gallery desk — wiped but not cleaned.

items.name: Silk Cravat
items.detail: A gentleman's neckcloth found near the body.

items.name: Arsenic Trioxide
items.detail: Compound sourced from palace crypt storage — not commercially obtained.

items.name: Protect a Political Mission
items.detail: Preserve infiltration of the court for revolutionary purposes.

items.name: Silence an Exposé
items.detail: Kill the one man who knew the false identity before he spoke.

items.name: Secure Imperial Favour
items.detail: Remove a rival for the Tsar's trust and patronage.

items.name: Conceal a Past Crime
items.detail: Prevent exposure of seditious activity in Moscow in 1914.

clues.heading: Kashin's Dossier
clues.text: The Okhrana's Moscow file on Dmitri Kashin — retrieved by telegraph — confirms he was wanted for organising illegal political assemblies in 1914. His last confirmed sighting was in St. Petersburg in early 1916, three months before Baron Sorin appeared at court. The file includes a physical description matching Sorin precisely.
clues.alone: Dmitri Kashin and Baron Sorin are the same man — the Okhrana file establishes identity, timeline, and prior criminal activity.

links.insight: Kashin's Okhrana dossier confirming his identity as Sorin and the arsenic trioxide traced to palace crypt stocks confirm he had both the political motive to silence Verin and access to the specific poison used.

clues.heading: The Poison's Origin
clues.text: Chemical analysis of the arsenic trioxide in Verin's glass matches a specific mineral purity consistent with a batch stored in the palace crypt's conservation laboratory — used for document preservation. The compound is not commercially available in this form. Access to the crypt requires a signed key requisition.
clues.alone: The poison was sourced from inside the palace's own crypt laboratory — only someone with key access could have obtained it.

links.insight: The arsenic sourced from the crypt laboratory and the key requisition log showing Sorin signed for crypt access two days before the murder confirm he obtained the poison in advance — the killing was planned at least 48 hours ahead.

clues.heading: Volkov and Drusin Cleared
clues.text: Countess Volkov's eleven-witness alibi in the Amber Antechamber is confirmed irrefutable by the palace majordomo's formal statement. Captain Drusin's west wing log is countersigned by three guards and corroborated by a supply delivery recorded at his post at 10 pm. Both suspects are eliminated.
clues.alone: Both Volkov and Drusin are definitively cleared — the field narrows to Sorin and anyone who assisted him.

links.insight: Volkov and Drusin formally cleared by physical evidence and Arkady Lenz's forged registry entry placing him as Sorin's co-conspirator confirm the killing involved exactly two people — the killer and the identity forger.

clues.heading: The Intercepted Letter
clues.text: Verin's letter to the chamberlain was opened, read, annotated in Sorin's ink, and resealed before Verin entered the gallery. The annotations include a question mark beside the name 'Kashin' and an underlined phrase — 'audience tomorrow' — suggesting Sorin understood the letter would reach the Tsar's office the following morning.
clues.alone: Sorin read the letter, identified the urgency of the timeline, and understood he had one evening to act before permanent exposure.

links.insight: Sorin's annotations on the intercepted letter establishing his awareness of the morning deadline and Verin's chamberlain appointment confirmed in the diary confirm Sorin acted with specific knowledge of the 12-hour window he had to prevent exposure.

clues.heading: The Crypt Key Requisition
clues.text: The palace crypt key requisition log shows Baron Sorin signed for access two days before the murder, citing 'document verification' as his purpose. No document verification was recorded by the crypt archivist. The key was returned the same day — but the arsenic batch log shows one vial unaccounted for.
clues.alone: Sorin obtained crypt access under a false pretext two days before the murder and removed a vial of arsenic trioxide from the conservation stock.

links.insight: Kashin's Okhrana dossier confirming his identity as Sorin and the arsenic trioxide traced to palace crypt stocks confirm he had both the political motive to silence Verin and access to the specific poison used.

clues.heading: Lenz and the Forgery
clues.text: Handwriting analysis of the forged genealogical entry for Baron Sorin confirms it was written by Arkady Lenz, the palace archivist. Lenz's own registry signature appears on three legitimate adjacent pages — the forged entry uses the same pen pressure and letter formation. When questioned, Lenz invokes his right to silence.
clues.alone: Arkady Lenz forged the genealogical entry that gave Sorin his false title — he is Sorin's co-conspirator in the identity fabrication.

links.insight: The arsenic sourced from the crypt laboratory and the key requisition log showing Sorin signed for crypt access two days before the murder confirm he obtained the poison in advance — the killing was planned at least 48 hours ahead.

clues.heading: Sorin's Exit Route
clues.text: A service passage behind the East Gallery's east wall — concealed behind a portrait panel — was found unlatched from inside. The passage leads to a courtyard gate that opens onto a canal-side street. A palace groundskeeper saw a man in a dark coat exit the courtyard gate at approximately the ninth-and-a-half hour of evening.
clues.alone: Sorin fled through a concealed service passage known only to staff and senior residents — someone showed him this route in advance.

links.insight: Volkov and Drusin formally cleared and Lenz's forged registry entry confirm the killing involved exactly two people — Sorin as the killer and Lenz as the identity architect who also revealed the escape route.

clues.heading: Verin's Appointment Diary
clues.text: Verin's personal diary, recovered from his coat, confirms a 9 pm appointment in the East Gallery — entered in his own hand that afternoon. The appointment is listed as 'S — private matter.' He had arranged to meet Sorin directly, apparently intending to give him the chance to withdraw before the letter reached the chamberlain.
clues.alone: Verin arranged the gallery meeting himself to give Sorin a final chance — he walked into a trap he unknowingly set.

links.insight: Sorin's annotations on the intercepted letter and Verin's diary appointment confirming he arranged the 9 pm meeting confirm Sorin used Verin's own gesture of mercy to control the location and timing of the killing.

detective.epilogue: Kashin — as Sorin — had six months of work to protect. Verin's offer of a private meeting was the perfect trap: a controlled location, no witnesses, and a poisoned glass prepared in advance from stolen palace stocks. Arkady Lenz had built the false identity and shown Sorin the escape route. Both were arrested — Sorin in Helsinki, Lenz three days later at the Warsaw train station.

inspector.title: The Romanov Mask
inspector.flavor: Kashin built nothing alone. The false identity, the palace access, the escape route — each required a hand inside the court. Lenz forged the registry. But someone with higher access smoothed Sorin's path to the Tsar's inner circle and kept the Okhrana from looking too closely. There are three people in this conspiracy, not two.
inspector.emptyState: Kashin killed. Lenz forged. Someone else opened every door.

items.name: Baron Alexei Sorin
items.detail: Dmitri Kashin — the killer operating under a stolen title.

items.name: Pavel Drusin
items.detail: Guard captain — his transfer from the front was sponsored.

items.name: Arkady Lenz
items.detail: Archivist — confirmed forger of the genealogical entry.

items.name: Father Grigory
items.detail: A Rasputin-adjacent mystic with unusual access to the Tsar.

items.name: The East Gallery
items.detail: Primary crime scene — poisoned glass, concealed exit.

items.name: The Amber Antechamber
items.detail: Volkov's alibi location — but her footman was not present all night.

items.name: The Palace Crypt
items.detail: Arsenic source — accessed by Sorin two days prior.

items.name: The Registry Archive
items.detail: Where Lenz forged Sorin's title and inserted the false entry.

items.name: The Tsar's Private Study
items.detail: Where Sorin's introduction letter originated — signed in error.

items.name: Poisoned Crystal Glass
items.detail: Arsenic trioxide in sherry — the confirmed cause of death.

items.name: Letter Opener
items.detail: Gallery desk blade — traces of blood on the hinge joint.

items.name: Silk Cravat
items.detail: Found near the body — secondary bruising on Verin's neck.

items.name: Arsenic Trioxide
items.detail: Sourced from crypt conservation — one vial unaccounted for.

items.name: Forged Document
items.detail: The false credentials that gave Sorin palace access and trust.

items.name: Forged the Identity
items.detail: Created the false genealogical entry and supporting papers.

items.name: Sponsored the Introduction
items.detail: Provided or facilitated the letter that brought Sorin to court.

items.name: Suppressed the Okhrana File
items.detail: Prevented Kashin's wanted status from surfacing during vetting.

items.name: Revealed the Escape Route
items.detail: Showed Sorin the concealed service passage before the murder.

items.name: Provided the Poison Access
items.detail: Arranged or enabled the crypt key requisition under false pretext.

items.name: Protect a Political Mission
items.detail: Preserve the revolutionary infiltration of the imperial court.

items.name: Silence an Exposé
items.detail: Prevent Verin's letter from reaching the chamberlain.

items.name: Secure Imperial Favour
items.detail: Remove a rival for the Tsar's patronage and trust.

items.name: Conceal a Past Crime
items.detail: Prevent exposure of seditious activity in Moscow in 1914.

items.name: Advance a Family Restoration
items.detail: Use the chaos of scandal to rehabilitate a fallen noble house.

clues.heading: The Introduction Letter
clues.text: Sorin's original introduction to the Tsar's court was carried by a letter of recommendation signed by the Tsar's chamberlain — but the chamberlain has no record of signing it. A graphologist confirms the signature is a skilled forgery. The letter's paper stock, however, is traced to a batch used exclusively in the Tsar's Private Study.
clues.alone: Sorin's introduction letter was forged on paper sourced from the Tsar's own study — someone with direct access to the imperial inner sanctum created it.

links.insight: The forged introduction letter on imperial study paper and Father Grigory's documented unsupervised access to the Tsar's Private Study confirm he was the one who created or facilitated the letter that placed Sorin inside the palace.

clues.heading: The Suppressed File
clues.text: The Okhrana's Moscow file on Dmitri Kashin was flagged for St. Petersburg distribution in January 1916 — three months before Sorin appeared at court. The distribution record shows the file was received by the palace security office but never logged in the vetting register. The receiving clerk's signature is illegible and does not match any current staff member.
clues.alone: Kashin's wanted file reached the palace and was deliberately suppressed — someone intercepted it before it could trigger a vetting review.

links.insight: The Okhrana file suppressed before vetting and Drusin's transfer sponsored by Father Grigory's ecclesiastical network confirm Grigory orchestrated both the security blind spot and the placement of a sympathetic guard captain to manage palace access.

clues.heading: The Footman's Second Account
clues.text: Countess Volkov's personal footman — initially placed with her in the Antechamber — admits under extended questioning that he left her service for forty minutes during the recital to carry a message. He delivered a sealed note to the East Gallery writing desk before Verin arrived. He cannot identify who instructed him to deliver it.
clues.alone: A sealed note was delivered to the East Gallery writing desk before Verin arrived — someone used Volkov's footman as an unwitting courier.

links.insight: The sealed note delivered to the gallery desk by Volkov's footman and the note's wax seal matching Father Grigory's personal signet recovered from his quarters confirm Grigory orchestrated Verin's final movements into the prepared trap.

clues.heading: Lenz's Handler
clues.text: A coded correspondence recovered from Lenz's archive desk, deciphered by the Okhrana, reveals he received instructions for the genealogical forgery from a contact identified only as 'the Father.' The letters reference payment in the form of ecclesiastical protection for Lenz's brother, a conscript facing disciplinary charges at the front.
clues.alone: Lenz forged Sorin's identity under instruction from a contact called the Father — who provided leverage through ecclesiastical influence over a military matter.

links.insight: Lenz's coded letters naming the Father as his handler and Father Grigory's documented intervention in eleven conscript cases through the same ecclesiastical channel confirm Grigory directed the entire identity construction operation.

clues.heading: The Private Study Paper
clues.text: The paper stock used in Sorin's forged introduction letter is identified as a specific imperial vellum watermarked with the Romanov double eagle — issued only to the Tsar's Private Study and handled by no more than four individuals. Father Grigory is documented as a frequent visitor to the study, with unsupervised access confirmed by two servants.
clues.alone: Father Grigory had unsupervised access to the imperial paper stock used in the forged introduction letter — he is the only non-staff visitor confirmed to have handled it.

links.insight: The forged introduction letter on imperial study paper and Father Grigory's documented unsupervised access to the Tsar's Private Study confirm he created or directly facilitated the letter that gave Sorin his entry to the palace.

clues.heading: Drusin's Transfer Sponsor
clues.text: Captain Drusin's transfer from the Galician front to the Winter Palace was approved through an unusual ecclesiastical petition — a letter from a monastic superior citing Drusin's spiritual necessity for palace duty. The monastic superior's office is linked in three prior cases to Father Grigory's personal correspondence network.
clues.alone: Drusin's palace posting was arranged through Father Grigory's ecclesiastical network — his placement was not coincidental.

links.insight: The Okhrana file suppressed before vetting and Drusin's transfer arranged through Grigory's network confirm Grigory managed both the information suppression and the personnel placement needed to keep Sorin's cover intact.

clues.heading: The Gallery Note
clues.text: The sealed note delivered to the East Gallery writing desk — recovered intact after the murder — contained only two lines: 'He will come at the ninth hour. The glass on the left.' The wax seal is a distinctive amber-coloured compound with an impression matching Father Grigory's personal signet ring, documented in a portrait hanging in the Amber Antechamber.
clues.alone: Father Grigory's personal seal is on the note that directed Sorin to poison Verin's specific glass — he coordinated the final act of the murder.

links.insight: The sealed note delivered to the gallery by Volkov's footman and the note's wax seal matching Grigory's personal signet confirm Grigory orchestrated the specific mechanics of Verin's killing, not merely the broader conspiracy.

clues.heading: Grigory's Conscript Files
clues.text: Palace records confirm Father Grigory personally intervened in the disciplinary cases of eleven conscripts between 1915 and 1916, securing favourable outcomes through informal ecclesiastical pressure on military tribunals. Lenz's brother appears as case nine. The pattern confirms Grigory routinely used conscript welfare as leverage to recruit palace insiders.
clues.alone: Father Grigory ran a systematic recruitment operation using conscript intervention as leverage — Lenz was one of at least eleven people he controlled this way.

links.insight: Lenz's coded letters naming the Father as his handler and Grigory's eleven documented conscript interventions through the same leverage model confirm Grigory directed the entire identity construction and murder operation from within the palace.

inspector.epilogue: Father Grigory had built a network of palace insiders over two years — conscripts' families as leverage, ecclesiastical authority as cover. He placed Kashin at court, suppressed the Okhrana file, arranged Drusin's transfer as a sympathetic guard, and directed Lenz to forge the identity. On the night of the murder he sent the gallery note through Volkov's unwitting footman, specifying the timing and the glass. Kashin did the killing. Grigory had constructed every condition that made it possible. All three were arrested. Grigory's trial was never made public.
